Bone Marrow Failure — The Empty Factory

🎯 Look at the lit symbol — say out loud what it is and what it represents (and the trap). Then reveal.

What it encodes
The board trap
Use ← → arrow keys · click a dot to jump · ▶ plays & narrates the scene
⚠️ Board traps 12
  1. BMF entrance hall — Failure of PRODUCTION (low reticulocytes, hypoproliferative) must be separated from peripheral DESTRUCTION/sequestration (high reticulocytes, hemolysis, hypersplenism) — checking the reticulocyte count is the first branch point and a perennial exam discriminator.
  2. Empty AA conveyor — Because acquired AA is immune-mediated, it RESPONDS to immunosuppression — distinguishing it from inherited (Fanconi/DC) and from MDS matters, since the latter need different therapy and AA-like immunosuppression can be harmful.
  3. Pancytopenia bins — Pancytopenia is NOT a single disease but a finding — don't equate it with aplastic anemia. An isolated single-lineage cytopenia (e.g., PRCA = red only) points to a completely different, more targeted differential.
  4. Hypocellular marrow — A hypocellular marrow is the OPPOSITE of MDS and myelophthisis, which are typically HYPERcellular (MDS) or packed/fibrotic (myelophthisis) — cellularity on biopsy is a key fork. Beware hypocellular MDS, a mimic that needs cytogenetics to unmask.
  5. AA exclusion sign — Skipping the exclusion workup — especially PNH flow cytometry and cytogenetics — is the classic error; a small PNH clone or an MDS clone in 'AA' changes prognosis and therapy.
  6. MDS dysplastic line — MDS is the great mimic of aplastic anemia but is typically HYPERcellular with dysplasia and clonal cytogenetics — missing it (and treating as AA) delays appropriate therapy (hypomethylating agents, lenalidomide for del(5q), transplant).
  7. Chromosome panels — A normal karyotype does not exclude MDS (single-lineage dysplasia or molecular mutations may still be present), and finding a clone in apparent AA reclassifies the disease — always send cytogenetics on the marrow.
  8. PRCA red-only belt — PRCA is single-lineage (red only) — calling it 'pancytopenia' is wrong. Its isolated reticulocytopenic anemia with normal WBC/platelets is the discriminator from aplastic anemia and MDS.
  9. Cellular catalog wall — Cytopenias + cellular marrow ≠ aplastic anemia — think MDS, B12/folate deficiency (megaloblastic), or hypersplenism instead. Reserve 'aplastic' for the genuinely hypocellular, fat-replaced marrow.
  10. Production failure poster — BMF is a production (output) problem — don't attribute pancytopenia to hemolysis or splenic sequestration without checking the reticulocyte count and spleen size; the reticulocyte index is the cheap, decisive first test.
  11. Crab-shaped shadow — Even acquired aplastic anemia carries a real risk of clonal evolution to MDS/AML and of harboring PNH clones — long-term monitoring is required, and a young AA patient still needs inherited-syndrome screening before transplant.
  12. Specialist desk — Peripheral blood alone cannot classify marrow failure — the aspirate may give a dry tap (fibrosis/infiltration), so a core biopsy is mandatory; never finalize the diagnosis on CBC and smear alone.
📖 Read-through — all 13 symbols
1. BMF entrance hall
Bone marrow failure is the unifying concept: the marrow factory fails to produce adequate mature blood cells, manifesting as one or more cytopenias. The central diagnostic question is WHY production is failing — empty marrow (aplastic anemia), dysplastic marrow (MDS), replaced marrow (myelophthisis), or selective failure of one lineage (PRCA). The smear plus the marrow biopsy partition these.
⚠️ Failure of PRODUCTION (low reticulocytes, hypoproliferative) must be separated from peripheral DESTRUCTION/sequestration (high reticulocytes, hemolysis, hypersplenism) — checking the reticulocyte count is the first branch point and a perennial exam discriminator.
2. Empty AA conveyor
Aplastic anemia is the prototype production failure: an empty, fat-replaced (hypocellular) marrow producing pancytopenia. Most cases are acquired and immune-mediated — cytotoxic T cells attack hematopoietic stem cells (often triggered by drugs, viruses like hepatitis/EBV, or idiopathic). Treatment is immunosuppression (ATG + cyclosporine) for older/no-donor patients, or allogeneic HSCT for young patients with a matched sibling donor.
⚠️ Because acquired AA is immune-mediated, it RESPONDS to immunosuppression — distinguishing it from inherited (Fanconi/DC) and from MDS matters, since the latter need different therapy and AA-like immunosuppression can be harmful.
3. Pancytopenia bins
Pancytopenia = simultaneous reduction of all three lineages: Red cells (anemia), White cells (leukopenia/neutropenia), and Platelets (thrombocytopenia). The differential spans decreased production (AA, MDS, myelophthisis, megaloblastic anemia, marrow infiltration) and increased destruction/sequestration (hypersplenism, PNH, autoimmune).
⚠️ Pancytopenia is NOT a single disease but a finding — don't equate it with aplastic anemia. An isolated single-lineage cytopenia (e.g., PRCA = red only) points to a completely different, more targeted differential.
4. Hypocellular marrow
The biopsy hallmark of aplastic anemia is a markedly hypocellular marrow (<25% cellularity, or <30% with severity criteria) in which fat replaces hematopoietic tissue, WITHOUT dysplasia, fibrosis, or infiltrate. Severity (Camitta criteria) is graded by neutrophil, platelet, and reticulocyte counts.
⚠️ A hypocellular marrow is the OPPOSITE of MDS and myelophthisis, which are typically HYPERcellular (MDS) or packed/fibrotic (myelophthisis) — cellularity on biopsy is a key fork. Beware hypocellular MDS, a mimic that needs cytogenetics to unmask.
5. AA exclusion sign
Aplastic anemia is a diagnosis of EXCLUSION. Before labeling it, rule out hypocellular MDS (cytogenetics, dysplasia), PNH (flow cytometry for CD55/CD59 loss), hairy cell leukemia (causes dry tap + monocytopenia), inherited syndromes (chromosome breakage, telomere length), and reversible causes (drugs, viral, nutritional).
⚠️ Skipping the exclusion workup — especially PNH flow cytometry and cytogenetics — is the classic error; a small PNH clone or an MDS clone in 'AA' changes prognosis and therapy.
6. Magnifying question
The diagnostic approach interrogates marrow architecture: is it EMPTY (aplastic), DYSPLASTIC (MDS), INFILTRATED/fibrotic (myelophthisis), or selectively absent in one lineage (PRCA)? CBC + smear + reticulocyte count narrow it, and the marrow aspirate/biopsy with cytogenetics and flow cytometry confirms.
7. MDS dysplastic line
Myelodysplastic syndromes are clonal stem-cell disorders of older adults featuring dysplastic, INEFFECTIVE hematopoiesis — peripheral cytopenias despite a typically HYPERcellular marrow (cells die before release). Findings include dysplastic morphology, ring sideroblasts, and clonal cytogenetic abnormalities (e.g., del(5q), -7). Carries a risk of transformation to AML; prognosis is stratified by IPSS-R.
⚠️ MDS is the great mimic of aplastic anemia but is typically HYPERcellular with dysplasia and clonal cytogenetics — missing it (and treating as AA) delays appropriate therapy (hypomethylating agents, lenalidomide for del(5q), transplant).
8. Chromosome panels
Cytogenetics and clonal markers are decisive in classifying marrow failure: an abnormal clonal karyotype (del(5q), monosomy 7, trisomy 8) signals MDS rather than idiopathic aplastic anemia, refines MDS prognosis, and helps detect clonal evolution. Flow cytometry for a PNH clone complements karyotyping.
⚠️ A normal karyotype does not exclude MDS (single-lineage dysplasia or molecular mutations may still be present), and finding a clone in apparent AA reclassifies the disease — always send cytogenetics on the marrow.
9. PRCA red-only belt
Pure red cell aplasia is selective failure of ONLY the erythroid line: severe anemia with reticulocytopenia but NORMAL white cells and platelets, and a marrow showing absent erythroid precursors with intact myeloid/megakaryocytic lineages. Causes include parvovirus B19, thymoma, drugs, autoimmune/lymphoproliferative disease (CLL, LGL leukemia), and anti-EPO antibodies.
⚠️ PRCA is single-lineage (red only) — calling it 'pancytopenia' is wrong. Its isolated reticulocytopenic anemia with normal WBC/platelets is the discriminator from aplastic anemia and MDS.
10. Cellular catalog wall
A normo- or hypercellular marrow in a patient with cytopenias points AWAY from aplastic anemia toward ineffective hematopoiesis (MDS, megaloblastic anemia), peripheral destruction/sequestration, or infiltration. Marrow cellularity is therefore a central triage feature.
⚠️ Cytopenias + cellular marrow ≠ aplastic anemia — think MDS, B12/folate deficiency (megaloblastic), or hypersplenism instead. Reserve 'aplastic' for the genuinely hypocellular, fat-replaced marrow.
11. Production failure poster
The first conceptual split in any cytopenia is reduced PRODUCTION versus increased peripheral DESTRUCTION/sequestration. Low reticulocyte count indicates a hypoproliferative/production problem (the BMF disorders); a high reticulocyte count points to hemolysis or blood loss; splenomegaly suggests sequestration.
⚠️ BMF is a production (output) problem — don't attribute pancytopenia to hemolysis or splenic sequestration without checking the reticulocyte count and spleen size; the reticulocyte index is the cheap, decisive first test.
12. Crab-shaped shadow
Behind acquired marrow failure lurk inherited/constitutional syndromes (DNA helix — Fanconi anemia, telomere disorders) and the risk of clonal evolution to MDS/AML (the crab = malignancy). Apparent 'idiopathic' AA can harbor a germline syndrome or evolve clonally over time, mandating ongoing surveillance.
⚠️ Even acquired aplastic anemia carries a real risk of clonal evolution to MDS/AML and of harboring PNH clones — long-term monitoring is required, and a young AA patient still needs inherited-syndrome screening before transplant.
13. Specialist desk
Definitive classification of bone marrow failure requires a hematologist-performed marrow ASPIRATE plus core BIOPSY, with morphology, cellularity assessment, cytogenetics, flow cytometry (PNH clone), and — when indicated — germline/molecular testing. This is what separates AA from MDS, myelophthisis, and PRCA.
⚠️ Peripheral blood alone cannot classify marrow failure — the aspirate may give a dry tap (fibrosis/infiltration), so a core biopsy is mandatory; never finalize the diagnosis on CBC and smear alone.
